site.title: The Rapaport Family — A Research Archive
site.subtitle: From Galicia to Brussels to Israel · 1888 — 2026
site.for_dov: For Dov Rapaport on his 80th birthday · August 2026
site.by_doron: Compiled by Dalia, Dana, Daniel and Doron Rapaport — with great help from Magda, Basia and Kasia

home.lead: An ongoing project to trace and tell the story of David and Lusha Rapaport — Holocaust survivors — and their son Bernard, born in Brussels in 1946.
home.intro_p1: What began as a few photographs and a memoir page became, through the work of a small team in Israel, Poland and France, an active research file: birth records pulled from Galician archives, a Brussels DIPIS card decoded line by line, a name written wrong on a wartime form, and a town called Mosina that may not be where anyone first thought.
home.intro_p2: This site is the research archive in its current state. Nothing is finished. Every entry is tagged with a level of confidence and the sources behind it. Open questions are listed openly. As findings come in, the site grows.
home.explore_tree: Walk the family tree, four generations on each side.
home.explore_timeline: Move through the years 1888 to today.
home.explore_hypotheses: See what we still don't know.

about.title: About this archive
about.intro: Created by the Rapaport family for their beloved Dov Rapaport on the occasion of his 80th birthday, August 2026.
about.method_p1: Every claim in the archive is tagged with a confidence level — confirmed (primary document), documented (stated in a document but not yet cross-checked), family memory (oral tradition), hypothesis (working theory), or ruled out. Sources are listed for every fact.
about.method_p2: Translations between Polish, French, English and Hebrew were made with the help of researchers Basia in Kraków, Kasia in Paris, and Ms. Kasia at the Jewish Historical Institute in Warsaw, alongside AI translation that has been spot-checked by humans.
about.thanks_p: To Magda, who connected the team. To Basia, who walked the Galician records. To Kasia, who broke the French DP card. To Ms. Kasia at ŻIH Warsaw, who found David's true birthplace. And to Dalia, Dana, and Daniel, who carried the memory.
